summary: Gruber analyzes Apple's M1 chip announcement, arguing it represents something far more ambitious than simply putting mobile chips in Macs — it's desktop-focused chip design leveraging Apple's silicon expertise. He emphasizes that battery life, not raw speed, is the killer feature for mass-market consumers, with the M1 MacBook Pro nearly doubling its Intel predecessor's battery life. He explains that all three M1 Macs are essentially the same computer in different form factors, and stresses that the M1's 16GB RAM cap doesn't signal regression — these are entry-level machines, with professional-grade Apple Silicon chips still to come. He dismisses comparisons to ARM-based Windows laptops as fundamentally misguided.
key_points: The M1 is a desktop-focused chip design, not a repurposed mobile chip — the difference between using mobile chips in desktops and using chip expertise to design desktop chips
Battery life is the mass-market selling point: the M1 MacBook Pro nearly doubles the Intel version's battery life, making charging and using truly separate activities like an iPad
All three M1 Macs are the same computer in different enclosures — the Pro adds a fan, brighter screen, and bigger battery but shares the same chip
The 16GB RAM and 2TB storage limits are M1-specific, not a regression — Apple continues selling Intel Macs for pro users and has yet to unveil professional-grade M-series chips
Comparing Apple Silicon to ARM-based Windows laptops is like comparing Teslas to electric scooters — Apple Silicon promises no trade-offs, not compromises
money_quotes: It's the difference between using their amazing mobile chips in desktops, and using their chip expertise to design desktop-focused chips.
Charge your MacBook Air or Pro overnight, and you can use it hard all day without ever once being near a power outlet.
There's no question in my mind that Apple could have made this transition to Apple Silicon a few years ago, but they clearly wanted to wait until the advantages were overwhelming and undeniable.
The mind-blowing part of all this is that the M1 is only being used in Apple's less expensive consumer Macs.
Pro, in Apple's product-naming parlance, doesn't always stand for professional. Much of the time it just means better or nicer.
Asking if M1 Macs are going to face the same problems as ARM-based Windows laptops is like asking if Teslas are going to hit the same limits as electric scooters.
It's like having your cake, eating it too, and getting to share your cake with your friends.
For over a decade, iPhones and iPads have had Apple-designed chips the competition could not and still cannot match. Now the Mac does too.
key_insight: The M1 isn't Apple putting phone chips in Macs — it's Apple applying a decade of mobile chip expertise to build purpose-designed desktop silicon, and the entry-level results are already so dominant that the professional-grade chips to come should terrify Intel.
